perturbation.original_text: The Company will promptly cause the Trustee, the Stock Purchase Contract Agent and the Collateral Agent to give the Remarketing Agents notice of all such defaults and events of which such trustee or agent is aware.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will endeavor to cause the Trustee, the Stock Purchase Contract Agent and the Collateral Agent to provide notice of defaults and events if they are reasonably able to do so.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear obligation for the Company to ensure prompt notice is given. The modified text replaces "will promptly cause" with "will endeavor to cause" and adds the condition "if they are reasonably able to do so," introducing ambiguity and weakening the obligation. "Endeavor" and "reasonably able" are subjective and difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts 15 U.S.C. § 77k, which outlines liability for false registration statements under the Securities Act of 1933. By weakening the obligation to promptly notify remarketing agents of defaults, the company potentially limits the agents' ability to conduct due diligence and be fully informed, thus increasing the risk of false or misleading information in the registration statement and violating securities law.

Civil liabilities on account of false registration statement (a) Persons possessing cause of action; persons liable In case any part of the registration statement, when such part became effective, contained an untrue statement of a material fact or omitted to state a material fact required to be stated therein or necessary to make the state
perturbation.justification: The original text creates a clear obligation for the Company to ensure prompt notice is given, but the changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation by replacing "will promptly cause" with "will endeavor to cause" and adding the condition "if they are reasonably able to do so." The LLM's law explanation states that by weakening the obligation to promptly notify remarketing agents of defaults, the company potentially limits the agents' ability to conduct due diligence and be fully informed, thus increasing the risk of false or misleading information in the registration statement and violating securities law. 15 U.S.C. § 77k, as indicated in the scraped law snippets, outlines liability for false registration statements under the Securities Act of 1933. The change from a clear obligation to a conditional and subjective one directly undermines the agents' ability to be fully informed, potentially leading to liability under 15 U.S.C. § 77k, if this lack of information contributes to a false registration statement. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly from time to time to take such action as the Remarketing Agents may reasonably request to qualify the Remarketed Securities for offering and sale under the securities laws of such jurisdictions as the Remarketing Agents may reasonably request and to comply with such laws so as to permit the continuance of sales and dealings therein in such jurisdictions for so long as may be necessary to complete the distribution of such Remarketed Securities, provided that in connection therewith the Company shall not be required to qualify as a foreign corporation, to file a general consent to service of process in any jurisdiction or to subject itself to taxation in respect of doing business in any jurisdiction in which it is not otherwise subject
perturbation.changed_text: The company will consider actions that the remarketing agents suggest in order to qualify the remarked securities for offering and sale under the securities laws of jurisdictions, and will aim to comply, but is only required to do so to the extent that its leadership approves.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates prompt action to qualify securities under applicable laws. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that the company will consider actions and will aim to comply, with the condition that its leadership approves. This makes compliance discretionary and less legally binding, as the approval of company leadership is subjective and lacks objective criteria.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change potentially contradicts Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 (15 U.S.C. § 77e), which requires registration of securities before offering or sale. By making the action to qualify the securities discretionary based on leadership approval, the company may fail to meet the registration requirements, violating federal securities laws.

Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails (a) Sale or delivery after sale of unregistered securities Unless a registration statement is in effect as to a security, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly- (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: s individual investors to bring civil actions under several provisions: Section 11 makes issuers strictly liable for registration statements that contain "an untrue statement of a material fact or omit to state a material fact required...to make the statements therein not misleading." Under this provision, a purchaser of the security can bring suit under Section 11, even if he bought the security after the initial offering, on the secondary markets. As long as the purchaser can trace the purchase back to the initial offering and is within the statute of limitations, he can sue; there is no need to prove causation or reliance on the misstatements or omissions. Damages are limited to the difference between the offering price and value of the securities at the time of the lawsuit. Although the purchaser can sue the issuer, underwriter, or subsequent seller, all defendants but the issuer have a “due diligence" defense that they had no grounds to believe the statement had a misstatement or omission. Section 5 and Section 12(a)(1) allow purchasers to sue sellers for offering or selling a non-exempt security without registering it. As long as the purchaser can prove a direct link between the purchaser and the seller, and the suit is within the statute of limitations, the purchaser may obtain rescission with interest, or damages if the investor sold his securities for less than he purchased them. Section 12(a)(2) creates liability for any person who offers or sells a security through a prospectus or an oral communication containing a material misstatement or omission . The person is liable to the purchaser for rescission of the purchase or damages, provided that the purchaser did not know about the misstatement or omission at the time of the purchase. Court holdings imply that the cause of action only applies to purchasers in the initial offering, not secondary purchases, but this is not settled law yet. Investors suing under 12(a)(2) can only recover from sellers. Section 15 aids investors by making "control persons," or persons who "control" defendants liable under Sections 11 and 12 by owning stock or under agency principals, jointly and severally liable . This helps investors collect damages in cases where the defendant is insolvent or does not have enough money to pay the investor, a frequent situation in securities litigation (most investors sue after their investment has soured). Section 17(a) is a key anti-fraud provision in the Securities Act. It provides for liability for fraudulent sales of securities. Some courts have found an implied right of private action under this provision, though this is becoming a less favored position. However, some courts continue to accept private suits under this provision. Section 17(a) makes it unlawful to "employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud", "obtain money or property" by using material misstatements or omissions, or to "engage in any transaction, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon the purchaser." This provision is closely tracked by Section 10b of the Securities Exchange Act and Rule 10b-5 , which is used more widely by investors suing for fraud. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by making compliance with securities laws discretionary, contingent upon leadership approval, whereas the original text mandated prompt action. This directly contradicts Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 (15 U.S.C. § 77e), as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, derived from 15 USC 77e, explicitly states the 'Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails' and indicates that 'Unless a registration statement is in effect as to a security, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly- (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce'. By allowing the company to potentially delay or avoid qualifying securities based on subjective internal approval, the modified text creates a situation where unregistered securities could be offered or sold, violating federal law. The introduction of ambiguity weakens the legally binding nature of the original obligation, undermining the registration requirements outlined in the Securities Act.
